1: "A life worthy of the calling" (ἀξίως τῆς κλήσεως) — the concept of living in conformity with one's status was a central concern of Hellenistic moral philosophy. Stoics spoke of living "according to nature" or "according to reason"; Aristotle's ethics centered on virtuous activity consistent with human excellence. Paul takes this form and fills it with a specifically covenantal content: the "calling" is divine election and the vocation it entails, not a philosophical ideal. The ethical imperative (chapters 4-6) rests on the indicative (chapters 1-3) — behavior follows from identity.
2: Humility (ταπεινοφροσύνη) was not a virtue in Greco-Roman culture — it was a vice, signifying low social status, servility, and the absence of the proper self-regard that a person of quality should maintain. Cicero, Plutarch, and Aristotle all treat excessive self-deprecation as a defect. Paul's elevation of humility as a virtue to be actively cultivated is one of the most counter-cultural moves in the NT ethical tradition — it derives from Jewish wisdom (Proverbs 3:34; Isaiah 57:15) and is embodied in Christ's self-emptying (Philippians 2:3-8).
3: "The bond of peace" (σύνδεσμος τῆς εἰρήνης) — the syndemos (bond, ligament) appears in Stoic cosmological thought as the rational principle that holds the universe together. Paul takes this philosophical cosmological language and applies it to the church community: peace functions as the structural bond of the new social order. The "unity of the Spirit" is not an achievement to be manufactured but a gift to be guarded — "keep" (τηρεῖν) implies preservation, not creation.
4: The seven unities of Ephesians 4:4-6 may reflect an early baptismal creed or catechetical formula used in the instruction of converts before baptism. Early Christian baptism was a public confession event at which the candidate declared their faith; the "one Lord, one faith, one baptism" structure mirrors the credal summaries that developed around baptismal practice (cf. 1 Corinthians 12:13; Galatians 3:27-28). "One hope when you were called" — the Greek "one hope of your calling" ties eschatological expectation to the moment of conversion.
5: "One Lord, one faith, one baptism" — the terse, staccato rhythm of these three phrases has a creedal quality. "One baptism" distinguished Christian practice from the many ritual washings of the Jewish world (cf. Hebrews 6:2, "instruction about baptisms" in the plural) and from John's baptism. The singularity of Christian baptism — unrepeatable, marking the irreversible transition of belonging — set it apart. The Didache (an early Christian document, c. AD 80-120) preserves debates about whether immersion or pouring was the proper mode, indicating that this one baptism was taken very seriously as a defining act.
6: "One God and Father of all, who is over all and through all and in all" — the threefold prepositional phrase (hyper, dia, en — over, through, in) echoes Stoic descriptions of the divine Logos permeating all things (cf. Marcus Aurelius; Seneca). Paul takes the Stoic cosmological idiom and applies it to the personal God of Israel. The Jewish confession of the divine unity (Deuteronomy 6:4, the Shema: "Hear, O Israel: the LORD our God, the LORD is one") underlies the "one God" claim, but now expanded to include the Father's relation to all people in Christ.
7: The transition from the seven unities to individual diversity in grace (Ephesians 4:7) mirrors the Jewish wisdom teaching that God gives different gifts to different persons (Wis Ephesians 7:7-8:1). Paul's "according to the measure of Christ's gift" presents Christ as the distributing patron who portions out grace-gifts to each member of the community proportionally — not equally, but appropriately.
8: The citation of Psalms 68:18 introduces a significant tradition-history question. The MT reads "you received gifts from men" (the ascending king receives tribute from the defeated); Paul reads "he gave gifts to men" (the ascending king distributes benefactions). The Syriac Peshitta and Aramaic Targum of Psalms 68:18 use a reading closer to Paul's, and the Targum was the standard Aramaic paraphrase used in synagogue worship — a form of the text Paul's Jewish audiences may have known. Whether Paul cites the Targum tradition, interprets the Hebrew with rabbinic flexibility, or follows a pre-existing Christian tradition is debated; what is clear is that the change is deliberate and interpretive.
9: The parenthetical explanation of "he ascended" implying a prior descent draws on Jewish cosmological thinking about the relationship between heaven and earth. The debate over what the descent refers to — incarnation (Irenaeus), descent into Hades (some ancient interpreters), or simply descent from heaven to earth at the incarnation — was active in the early church. Jewish apocalyptic literature described prophets ascending to heaven (1 Enoch 14; 2 Enoch 3-7) and returning; Paul's pattern of descent-then-ascent follows this pattern with Christ as the one who moves between realms definitively.
10: "In order to fill the whole universe" — tōn ouranōn (the heavens) in the plural echoes Jewish cosmological descriptions of multiple heavens (2 Corinthians 12:2; 2 Enoch 1-20; T. Levi 2-3), through all of which Christ has ascended to the highest position. The concept of the exalted Lord filling all cosmic space echoes Jewish wisdom traditions where divine Wisdom filled all things (Wis Ephesians 7:24; Sir Ephesians 24:3-7); Paul presents Christ as the one who supplants Wisdom as the cosmic ordering principle, now filling all creation from the highest heaven to the lowest earth.
11: "Apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors and teachers" — the list reflects the early church's functional structure rather than a fixed ecclesiastical hierarchy. Apostles and prophets appear to be itinerant, establishing churches and speaking revelatory words; evangelists similarly mobile; pastors and teachers likely resident in local communities. The single Greek article governing "pastors and teachers" (τοὺς δὲ ποιμένας καὶ διδασκάλους) has led many commentators to understand these as a single linked role — the "pastor-teacher" — rather than two separate offices.
12: "Works of service" (ergon diakoniās) — diakonia (service) carried connotations of servant work in the Greco-Roman social world; it was associated with low status and was not the language of leadership. Paul's elevation of service as the purpose of the gifted leaders — they equip the whole people for diakonia — is counter-cultural. The leaders serve the servants; the goal is the building up of the body, not the display of leadership competence.
13: "Mature" (teleios) was a term in Hellenistic philosophy (Stoic, Platonic) for the philosophically perfected person who had achieved the complete development of human reason and virtue — the sage, the Stoic wise man. Paul uses the term for corporate rather than individual maturity: the goal is that "we all" reach teleiotēs together, "in the unity of the faith." The Stoic ideal was the solitary wise man; Paul's goal is a community that together reaches full stature in Christ.
14: "Infants, tossed about by the waves" — the image of a ship driven by wind and wave was a standard Hellenistic philosophical metaphor for a person lacking stable wisdom (Plato, Republic 488a-e; Philo, On the Migration of Abraham 148). The technical terms in the next phrase are notable: kubeia (dice-playing) denoted cunning manipulation and sharp practice; methodia tēs planēs (scheming of error/deceit) uses the word for wandering (planē) that gives us "planet" — the wandering stars that were seen as unstable and potentially malevolent. False teaching is described through imagery of cosmic instability.
15: "Speaking the truth in love" (ἀληθεύοντες ἐν ἀγάπῃ) — in Hellenistic friendship ethics, frank speech (parrēsia) was the virtue of the true friend who told you what you needed to hear, rather than what you wanted to hear — flattery being the mark of the false friend (Plutarch, How to Tell a Flatterer from a Friend). Paul adopts this framework but fills it with love as the governing motive. The goal of truthful speech is not philosophical correction but the growth of the whole body into Christ.
16: The body metaphor with joints (haphē) and ligaments (syndesmos) engaging each part draws on the ancient medical tradition. Galen and Hippocratic writers described the body's unity through the interdependence of its parts — a wound to one member affects the whole. The Stoics used body-as-cosmos imagery to argue for the interconnectedness of all things. Paul applies this medical and philosophical body-language to the church, but gives it a distinctive head: the body grows only "from him" (ἐξ οὗ — from whom, i.e., from Christ).
17: "The futility of their thinking" (ματαιότης τοῦ νοὸς) — the term mataiotēs (futility, vanity) is the word used throughout Ecclesiastes (LXX) for the emptiness of life under the sun. In Jewish polemical tradition, Gentile idolatry was characterized as thinking that leads nowhere — the mind worships what cannot see or speak or act, and is consequently darkened (cf. Wis Ephesians 13:1-9; Romans 1:21). Paul's "Gentile mind" is a forensic category — what they were before conversion — not a permanent ethnic characteristic.
18: "Hardened in heart" (pōrōsis tēs kardias) echoes the OT tradition of divine hardening as judgment on persistent rejection: pharaoh's heart (Exodus 9:12); Israel's hardening in Isaiah 6:10 (cited in all four Gospels and Acts 28). The heart in Hebrew anthropology is the seat of will and moral cognition; a hardened heart is one that has lost the capacity to respond to what it knows. Moral insensitivity and cognitive blindness are two aspects of the same condition — willful rejection leading to incapacity.
19: "Having lost all sensitivity" (ἀπηλγηκότες) — the verb means to have become calloused, to have ceased to feel pain. The medical image is precise: a part of the body that has been repeatedly damaged may lose its pain receptors and can no longer register further injury. The Stoics described the morally degraded person similarly — unable to feel the sting of conscience. Having given themselves over to sensuality, these persons have entered a self-reinforcing cycle: insensitivity enables indulgence, which deepens insensitivity.
20: "That, however, is not the way of life you learned" — the emphatic "you" (ὑμεῖς δέ) marks the contrast with the Gentile lifestyle just described. The formula "learned Christ" (ἐμάθετε τὸν Χριστόν) is unusual — one learns a teacher's doctrine, not the teacher himself. Paul's phrasing suggests that Christ is not merely the subject of instruction but the content and the teacher simultaneously.
21: "Were taught in him in accordance with the truth that is in Jesus" — the vocabulary of learning (μαθεῖν), hearing (ἀκοῦσαι), and being taught (διδαχθῆναι) is the technical language of discipleship and catechesis in the ancient synagogue and early church. The pattern — learn from a teacher, hear the tradition, be formally instructed — was how moral formation worked in both Jewish and Hellenistic education. Paul presents conversion as entry into a comprehensive educational formation, not merely a change of belief.
22: "Put off your old self" — the clothing metaphor for moral transformation was common in both Jewish and Hellenistic moral literature. In Jewish tradition, the high priest's garments symbolized moral status (Zechariah 3:3-5); Isaiah uses clothing for righteousness and salvation (Isaiah 61:10). In Hellenistic tradition, Stoic writers described the philosopher "putting on" virtue and "taking off" vice. Early Christian baptism was accompanied by an actual change of garment, making the metaphor liturgically enacted as well as conceptually expressed.
23: "Be made new in the attitude of your minds" (ἀνανεοῦσθαι τῷ πνεύματι τοῦ νοὸς ὑμῶν) — the renewal of the nous (mind, rational faculty) directly contrasts with the "futility of their thinking" (mataiotēs tou noos) in Ephesians 4:17. The transformation Paul envisions is cognitive and volitional, not merely behavioral — it is the mental frame that generates the behavior, and it must be continuously renewed (present passive infinitive: ongoing action, not a single event).
24: "The new self, created to be like God in true righteousness and holiness" — the "new self" language evokes the creation of humanity in God's image (Genesis 1:26-27). The Fall had marred that image; the new creation in Christ restores it. The pair dikaiosunē (righteousness) and hosiotēs (holiness/piety) is the standard Greek ethical pair for justice toward fellow humans and piety toward God — found in Plato, Thucydides, and in Jewish writings (cf. Luke 1:75). Paul's "new self" is not a privatized spiritual state but an embodied social and relational reality.
25: "We are all members of one body" — the mele (members) language returns to the body metaphor of Ephesians 4:12-16. In Roman legal theory, members of the same corpus (corporation, association) had mutual obligations defined by their shared membership. Paul grounds the obligation to truthful speech not in general ethics but in ecclesial identity: lying to fellow church members is like a body part deceiving itself.
26: "In your anger do not sin" is a citation of Psalms 4:4 (LXX). The Psalm was an evening prayer (its heading notes it was "for the director of music"), and the following verse about "while you are on your beds" supports reading this as advice not to let anger carry over through the night. The principle "do not let the sun go down on your anger" may echo Deuteronomy 24:15, which forbids withholding a laborer's wages overnight — a similar principle of not allowing unfulfilled obligations to accumulate past the day's end.
27: "Do not give the devil a foothold" — topos (place, opportunity) is both a military and a legal term: a topos is a strategic position in battle, or a ground for legal action. The image is tactical: allowing anger to persist overnight gives the adversary an established position from which to launch further operations. The identification of the adversary as "the devil" (ὁ διάβολος) uses the Greek term for the accuser or slanderer — the one who operates through accusation and deception.
28: Manual labor was despised by the social elite in Greco-Roman society as incompatible with the dignity of a gentleman. Cicero (De Officiis I.150-151) lists tradesmen and manual workers among those whose occupation "cannot be gentlemanly" because it requires selling labor. Jewish tradition had a different evaluation — the rabbis worked trades alongside teaching (Paul himself was a tentmaker). Paul's elevation of hand-work as the means of earning and of giving to those in need is counter-cultural relative to Greco-Roman social values.
29: "Unwholesome talk" — sapros (rotten, decayed) was used for food that had spoiled and for wood that had rotted. Applied to speech, it signals talk that corrupts rather than nourishes. The positive alternative — speech that "ministers grace" to hearers — uses the charis vocabulary that runs through Ephesians: even conversation participates in the economy of grace or its opposite. The "need" of the hearer, not the preference of the speaker, determines what is appropriate.
30: "Do not grieve the Holy Spirit" — the background is Isaiah 63:10: "they rebelled and grieved his Holy Spirit." Isaiah's account of Israel's rebellion in the wilderness becomes Paul's warning to the Ephesian community. The verb lypeite (grieve) implies that the Spirit is a personal agent who can be hurt by behavior — one grieves a person, not a force or an influence. "Sealed for the day of redemption" returns to the seal language of Ephesians 1:13-14: the Spirit's presence is the mark of divine ownership, guaranteed until the final redemption.
31: "Bitterness, rage, anger, brawling, slander, along with every form of malice" — this vice list (pikria, thymos, orgē, kraugē, blasphēmia, kakia) follows a conventional Hellenistic and Jewish pattern of moral instruction. Vice and virtue lists were standard teaching forms in Stoic diatribe, Jewish wisdom literature (Wis Ephesians 14:25-26; T. Gad Ephesians 5:1), and early Christian catechesis (Colossians 3:8; Galatians 5:19-21). The listing itself was a mnemonic form, intended to be memorized and reviewed in moral self-examination.
32: "Forgiving each other, just as in Christ God forgave you" — Hellenistic moral philosophy encouraged forgiveness as a virtue of the magnanimous person: the truly great-souled (μεγαλόψυχος) person does not hold grudges because they consider offenses beneath their dignity. Paul's ground is entirely different: forgiveness is grounded in the prior forgiveness received from God, not in personal nobility or the insignificance of the offense. The "just as" (καθώς) is normative and specific — the scope and character of divine forgiveness in Christ sets the pattern for human forgiveness.
